title: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
summary: Use git mv and git rm so a project cleanup is immediately represented in the staging area.

sketchBlocks.title: How to Rename Files in Git
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Renaming a file in Git is more than just changing its name in the filesystem. Git needs to track this change to maintain the history of the file. The `git mv` command is used to rename files while keeping Git informed.

Worked example: Suppose you have a file named `old_name.txt` that you want to rename to `new_name.txt`. You can achieve this by running the command:

```bash
git mv old_name.txt new_name.txt
```

This command renames the file and stages the change for the next commit. It ensures that the rename is tracked in the version history, preserving the file's history under the new name.

sketchBlocks.title: How to Remove Files in Git
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When a file is no longer needed, you can remove it from your project using Git. The `git rm` command removes the file from the working directory and stages the deletion for the next commit.

Worked example: If you have a file named `obsolete_file.txt` that you want to remove, you can use the following command:

```bash
git rm obsolete_file.txt
```

This command deletes the file and stages the removal. The file will be removed from the repository in the next commit, ensuring that the deletion is tracked in the version history.

sketchBlocks.title: Check Staged Renames and Deletions
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Before committing changes, it's crucial to verify what has been staged. The `git status` command provides a summary of changes in the working directory and staging area.

Worked example: After renaming or removing files, you can check the status with:

```bash
git status
```

This command will show renamed and deleted files under the 'Changes to be committed' section, allowing you to confirm that the intended changes are staged correctly.

sketchBlocks.title: Ignoring Unwanted Files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `.gitignore` file is used to specify files and directories that Git should ignore. This is useful for excluding temporary files, build artifacts, or other files that should not be tracked.

Worked example: To ignore all `.log` files in your project, add the following line to your `.gitignore` file:

```
*.log
```

This ensures that any file with a `.log` extension will not be tracked by Git, keeping your repository clean from unnecessary files.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of git mv
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git mv` command?
options: To rename files and stage the change
To delete files from the repository
quizDraft.hint: Think about how Git tracks file changes.

help.concept: The `git mv` command is used to rename files while keeping Git informed of the change.
help.hint_1: It involves renaming files in a way that Git can track.
help.hint_2: It stages the rename for the next commit.

quizDraft.prompt: What happens when you use the `git rm` command?

The file is deleted and the deletion is staged

quizDraft.title: Order of Operations for Renaming a File
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps to rename a file in Git correctly.
tokens: Check current status with git status
Rename the file with git mv
correctOrder: Check current status with git status
Rename the file with git mv
quizDraft.hint: Consider the sequence from checking the status to committing the change.

help.concept: Renaming a file involves using `git mv`, checking the status, and committing the change.
help.hint_1: Start with checking the current status.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then complete the requested Git task.
quizDraft.instructions:
Create a `.gitignore` file to ignore all `.tmp` files in the repository.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then complete the requested Git task.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
git init -q -b main
git config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '.zoeskoul/
main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Git practice file 4\n' > practice-4.txt

solutionFiles.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash

' >> .git/info/exclude
